    Chapter 9

    Chapter 9: ROS 2 Debugging Tools¶

    Introduction¶

    Now you get a solid fundation on all the mian topic of ROS2 but one of the mai phase of robot programming is actually debug, and ROS know this, infact it gives to you several ways to debug what you are doing. In this tutorial we will see someone.

    ROS2 Debug message with rtq_console¶

    Every time you use self.get_logger().info(), warn(), or error(), ROS 2 does two things:

    1. It prints the string to your terminal.
    2. It publishes the string as a standardized message to a hidden topic called /rosout. When you have 20 nodes running across 3 different computers, looking at one terminal is useless and ineffective. We use a GUI tool called rqt_console to aggregate, filter, and search through every log message generated by every node in the network.

    In a new terminal, run the following command to open the graphical console:

    In [ ]:
    Copied! 
    ros2 run rqt_console rqt_console
    ros2 run rqt_console rqt_console

    a window will open just like this:

    if you run the action server just defined and you open it again you will get this:

    in this console we can see our server running, we can filter the message recived based on INFO, WARN, DEBUG, ERROR, which are the possible parameters when we write: self.get_logger().info() ect.

    RVIZ¶

    RVIZ is a data visualizer, it knows nothings about physics or robots but it can only drwas what ROS2 nodes is telling to the networks via topics or transforms. you already lunched a setupped version of RVIZ previously but if you want a clean window you need to type rviz2 in your terminal.

    Visualize the robot frames with TF¶

    The most critical debugging tool in ROS 2 is the Transform Tree (TF2). A robot is made of a lots of links (base, wheels, laser, camera). TF2 keeps track of where every part is relative to every other part mathematically. If your laser scanner is working, but it thinks it is attached to the robot's rear instead of the front, your robot will drive backward into walls. Let's visualize the "Skeleton" of our amrj16 robot in RViz!

    Follow this steps:

    In [ ]:
    Copied! 
    #open gazebo with
!ros2 launch amrj16_gazebo start_world.launch.py

#open rviz with
!ros2 launch amrj16_description urdf_amrj16_visualize.launch.py
    #open gazebo with !ros2 launch amrj16_gazebo start_world.launch.py #open rviz with !ros2 launch amrj16_description urdf_amrj16_visualize.launch.py

    Now go to the left side panel and search for the TF tranform section, open it and flag the show names option and open the frame section, you will have something like this:

    As you can see th ename of the frames are appeard and on the left panel you can see the tree of this frames, how they are connect and to which are connect.

    This is one of the hundreds of visualization that Rviz can do.

    ROS2 doctor¶

    When things are getting bad, there is one tool that perform several checks to ensure everything is correct, this is called ros2 doctor. It analyzes your environment variables, network configuration, and package installations to find common errors. If ros2 doctor returns User WARNING or ERROR, it will usually tell you exactly what environment variable is missing or if multiple machines are colliding on the same DDS network domain.

    To check the command is:

    In [ ]:
    Copied! 
    !ros2 doctor
    !ros2 doctor

    and in a healty environment you should get something like this:

    Of course is truncated because the cheks are multiples and doesn't fit in one single window. But if you get the line: "All 5 checks passed" your system is in a good shape.

    Made with Material for MkDocs
